title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a local arts festival. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Nina, The Ticket Desk Volunteer
Nina joined the arts festival after promising herself she would spend less time working from home and more time meeting neighbours. The committee gave her the ticket desk because she seemed calm with numbers. [47]Her first problem was discovering that two separate lists of paid bookings had been printed, so several seats appeared to have been sold twice.[/47] She spent an evening comparing names before anyone noticed. [48]Her background as a bookkeeper proved useful when she had to balance cash payments, online receipts and complimentary tickets without losing track.[/48] She expected the desk to be boring, yet it became the place where lost performers, nervous parents and confused visitors all stopped first. Nina says she learned to give short, confident answers, even when she was not completely sure. By the final weekend, she was enjoying the small ceremony of handing over a ticket and watching people step into the hall.
texts.B:
### Marcus, The Stage Runner
[49]Marcus became involved when his neighbour, who managed the theatre, asked him to help backstage for one weekend and then kept calling him back.[/49] He had imagined carrying chairs, but the job was much more varied. [50]He was surprised by the number of tiny last-minute requests performers made, from darker water bottles to a particular kind of tape for their shoes.[/50] At first he found this irritating, until an actor explained that familiar details helped people feel steady before going on stage. Marcus liked being invisible while making the show possible. He learned the quickest routes between dressing rooms, storage cupboards and the side door used by musicians. Once, when a microphone stand disappeared, he borrowed one from a school band across the road just in time. He says the applause felt partly his, although nobody in the audience knew his name. That quiet ownership was completely new to him.
texts.C:
### Priya, The Window Poster Designer
Priya, a freelance illustrator, volunteered after noticing how many empty shop windows lined the street leading to the festival venue. [51]While waiting for the bus each morning, she kept imagining those blank panes filled with colour instead of old rental notices.[/51] The organisers had no budget for advertising, so she designed posters that could be printed cheaply and rearranged in different windows. [55]She created a set of simple symbols so that passers-by could identify music, theatre, film and children’s events without reading the whole programme.[/55] [52]The low point came when rain leaked through one window frame and ruined a complete display overnight.[/52] Priya considered giving up on paper altogether, but a café owner offered plastic sleeves and helped her rehang everything. She says the project taught her to design for real streets, not just clean computer screens, and to accept that public work has to survive weather, fingerprints and curiosity.
texts.D:
### Daniel, The Access Guide
[53]Daniel offered to review the festival site because his sister, who uses a wheelchair, had stopped attending events there after several frustrating visits.[/53] He did not want the organisers to feel accused, so he began by walking through the route with them and pointing out where small changes would help. [54]His most delicate task was mapping entrances, toilets and seating areas without directing visitors through steps, loose cables or crowded backstage corridors.[/54] He also wrote brief notes explaining which performances had strong lighting or sudden noise. Some volunteers were embarrassed when he mentioned problems they had never noticed, but Daniel tried to keep the tone practical. He was proud when his sister stayed for an entire concert for the first time in years. [56]He is now preparing a checklist for next year’s team so the access work will not depend on his personal availability.[/56] For him, inclusion means planning before anyone has to ask.

questions.question: had to deal with an administrative mistake that could have affected people’s bookings?
questions.explanation: Nina discovered that two separate lists of paid bookings had been printed, making some seats appear sold twice.

questions.question: says a professional skill helped them keep financial records accurate?
questions.explanation: Nina’s bookkeeping background helped her balance cash payments, online receipts and complimentary tickets.

questions.question: was recruited by a neighbour who was already responsible for the venue?
questions.explanation: Marcus became involved when his neighbour, the theatre manager, asked him to help backstage.

questions.question: was surprised by the number of detailed requests made shortly before a performance?
questions.explanation: Marcus was surprised by the tiny last-minute requests performers made.

questions.question: got the idea after repeatedly noticing unused display spaces while waiting for transport?
questions.explanation: Priya imagined empty shop windows filled with colour while waiting for the bus.

questions.question: describes a setback caused by damage to a completed display?
questions.explanation: Rain leaked through a window frame and ruined Priya’s display overnight.

questions.question: became involved because a relative had previously found the venue difficult to use?
questions.explanation: Daniel reviewed the site because his sister had stopped attending after frustrating visits.

questions.question: had to plan routes that avoided physical barriers and hazards?
questions.explanation: Daniel mapped entrances, toilets and seating without directing visitors through steps, cables or crowded corridors.

questions.question: designed visual information to help people understand event types quickly?
questions.explanation: Priya created simple symbols for music, theatre, film and children’s events.

questions.question: is preparing a practical tool so future volunteers can continue the work without them?
questions.explanation: Daniel is preparing a checklist for next year’s access team.
